currentUse: Active Roman Catholic parish church — Our Lady of Sorrows, 136 Davis Ave., Kearny, NJ (Archdiocese of Newark), founded 1915 as Our Lady of Sorrows Lithuanian R.C. Church; still holding daily and Sunday Masses
summary: Our Lady of Sorrows at 136 Davis Ave., Kearny — founded in 1915 as "Our Lady of Sorrows Lithuanian R.C. Church" to serve roughly 700 Lithuanians in Harrison and 400 in Kearny — is standing and operating as an active Archdiocese of Newark parish, with daily Mass Monday–Friday at 7:00 AM, a 5:00 PM Saturday vigil, and 9:00 AM Sunday Mass. The building retains its Lithuanian fabric: a plaque marking it as officially Lithuanian, a 1962 Knights of Lithuania sun-cross at the entrance, stained glass of Our Lady of Vilna and Our Lady of Šiluva, Lithuanian-inscribed Stations of the Cross, and pews bearing donor Lithuanian family names. The parish is small and shrinking: archdiocesan planning data records Mass attendance of 178 in 2019 falling to 164 in 2024, with 2 baptisms against 11 funerals in 2024, and weekly collections in the $800–1,000 range per the August 2025 bulletin. Since July 1, 2024 it has shared its pastor, Fr. Cesar J. Quinones, with neighboring St. Cecilia, Kearny (appointment runs to June 30, 2030), and its deacon is John Sarnas with parish council coordinator Linda Mackalonis — Lithuanian surnames still visible in parish leadership and Mass intentions. The parish is exposed to the archdiocese's "We Are His Witnesses" restructuring: it sits in Planning Area I (Southeast Essex), and both Round 1 draft pastorate models published in September 2025 group Our Lady of Sorrows with St. Cecilia (one option adds the two Harrison parishes); the archdiocese states the process "is not a plan to close or merge parishes" but draft models were in parish listening sessions as of late 2025. As of mid-July 2026 the parish website carried a notice that the July 11–12 weekend Masses would be celebrated at St. Cecilia instead of Our Lady of Sorrows "due to unforeseen circumstances," with no reason given. No Lithuanian-language Mass appears on the published schedule, and the parish today describes itself as "comprised of many families from different cultures and backgrounds."

developments.headline: Pastor now shared with St. Cecilia, Kearny
developments.detail: The Archdiocese of Newark appointed Father Cesar Quinones pastor of both Our Lady of Sorrows and St. Cecelia, Kearny, effective July 1, 2024, with a term ending June 30, 2030 — the two Kearny parishes now operate under one pastor. The parish staff page also lists Rev. Octavio Gonzalez as parochial vicar and Deacon John Sarnas.

sources.title: Parish Staff – Our Lady of Sorrows Kearny
sources.publisher: Our Lady of Sorrows Kearny (parish website)

developments.headline: Active but small parish life; new reduced Mass schedule
developments.detail: The August 3, 2025 parish bulletin shows a functioning parish with daily 7:00 AM Masses Monday–Friday, a 5:00 PM Saturday vigil and 9:00 AM Sunday Mass, and announces a 'NEW MASS SCHEDULE — No Saturday 7:00 a.m. Mass.' Weekly collections were $997 (first) and $275 (second) on 7/20/25 and $819 on 7/27/25. Lithuanian surnames (Daunoras, Mackalonis, Struzienski) appear throughout Mass intentions and prayer lists; the bulletin is in English only, with no Lithuanian-language Mass listed.

sources.title: Our Lady of Sorrows Kearny parish bulletin, week of August 3, 2025

developments.headline: Named in archdiocesan 'We Are His Witnesses' restructuring draft models
developments.detail: Our Lady of Sorrows is in Planning Area I—Southeast Essex (29 parishes, 44 priests) of the Archdiocese of Newark's 'We Are His Witnesses' pastoral planning process, which held parish listening sessions on draft models. The Round 1 pastorate-model document (posted September 2025) records Our Lady of Sorrows Mass attendance of 178 in 2019 vs. 164 in 2024 (-8%), 2 baptisms and 11 funerals in 2024, and in both Option A and Option B groups it with St. Cecilia, Kearny (Option A adds Holy Cross and Our Lady of Czestochowa, Harrison). The archdiocese states the process 'is not a plan to close or merge parishes,' but the parish's pastorate consolidation exposure is explicit.

sources.title: Planning Process – We Are His Witnesses
Pastorate Models: Round 1 — Planning Area: Southeast Essex (I)

developments.headline: Weekend Masses of July 11–12 temporarily relocated to St. Cecilia
developments.detail: As of mid-July 2026 the parish homepage carries a notice: 'Due to unforeseen circumstances, the Masses for Sunday, July 11 & 12 will not be celebrated at Our Lady of Sorrows Church,' redirecting parishioners to St. Cecilia Church, 120 Kearny Ave (5 PM Saturday vigil, 10:30 AM Sunday, both English). No reason for the relocation is given. The same page confirms the regular schedule (daily 7 AM Mon–Fri, Sat vigil 5 PM, Sun 9 AM) and describes today's parish as 'comprised of many families from different cultures and backgrounds.'

New Jersey: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage
Our Lady of Sorrows — Kearny, NJ

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions. The archive's core facts are confirmed: the church stands, remains diocese-owned (Archdiocese of Newark), is still marked as officially Lithuanian (plaque per Global True Lithuania), and holds daily Masses (Mon–Fri 7:00 AM per parish website and August 2025 bulletin). Refinements: (1) the archive's 2022 characterization as 'one of the strongest Lithuanian bastions in New Jersey' holds only relative to other NJ Lithuanian parishes' fates — the parish today self-describes as multicultural ('many families from different cultures and backgrounds'), publishes no Lithuanian-language Mass, and Lithuanian identity persists mainly in the building's fabric and parishioner surnames (Daunoras, Mackalonis, Sarnas); (2) archdiocesan data quantifies decline the archive doesn't capture: Mass attendance 164 in 2024 (down 8% from 178 in 2019), 2 baptisms vs. 11 funerals in 2024; (3) new restructuring exposure post-dating the archive: pastor shared with St. Cecilia since July 2024, and both September 2025 'We Are His Witnesses' draft pastorate models group it with St. Cecilia. Also, the parish website states founding in 1915 (archive has 'not stated').
gaps: Whether any Lithuanian-language liturgy, Lithuanian Mass, or organized Lithuanian community group (e.g., Knights of Lithuania council) still operates at the parish could not be determined — the published schedule, events page, and August 2025 bulletin are English-only with no Lithuanian references beyond surnames; searched 'Kearny Our Lady of Sorrows Lithuanian Mass 2024/2025/2026' with no results. The reason for the July 11–12, 2026 relocation of weekend Masses to St. Cecilia ('unforeseen circumstances') is unstated — whether it is building-related is unknown. No secular press coverage of the parish from 2024–2026 was found; all recent material is parish or diocesan. The outcome of the 'We Are His Witnesses' Round 1 draft models (final pastorate decisions, implementation dates) had not been published as of the search. The parish website's HTTPS certificate has expired, which may indicate limited administrative resources but is not itself evidence of parish status.
